---
title: "Time Series Analysis in R: Environmental Examples"
author: "Your Name"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
---

# Introduction

Environmental data often shows strong temporal patterns. This tutorial demonstrates time series analysis in R using environmental datasets:

- Atmospheric CO2 (Keeling Curve)
- Global temperature anomalies (NASA GISTEMP)
- Seasonal decomposition
- Forecasting models

All code runs on RStudio Server and the resulting HTML can be published on GitHub Pages.

---

# Setup

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(tsibble)
library(feasts)
library(fable)
library(readr)
```

---

# 1. Keeling Curve (CO2 at Mauna Loa)

```r
co2_url <- "https://gml.noaa.gov/webdata/ccgg/trends/co2/co2_mm_mlo.csv"

co2 <- read_csv(
  co2_url,
  comment = "#",
  col_names = c("year","month","decimal_date","average",
                "interpolated","trend","days")
)

co2_ts <- co2 %>%
  mutate(date = ymd(paste(year, month, 15, sep = "-"))) %>%
  as_tsibble(index = date)
```

## Plot CO2 trend

```r
co2_ts %>%
  ggplot(aes(date, trend)) +
  geom_line(color = "firebrick") +
  labs(title = "Keeling Curve (Trend Component)",
       y = "CO2 (ppm)", x = "Year")
```

---

# 2. Seasonal Decomposition

```r
co2_ts %>%
  model(STL(trend ~ season(window = "periodic"))) %>%
  components() %>%
  autoplot()
```

---

# 3. Global Temperature Anomalies

```r
temp_url <- "https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/tabledata_v4/GLB.Ts+dSST.csv"

temp_raw <- read_csv(temp_url, skip = 1)

temp <- temp_raw %>%
  select(Year, Jan:Dec) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = Jan:Dec, names_to = "month", values_to = "anomaly") %>%
  mutate(month = match(month, month.abb),
         date = ymd(paste(Year, month, 1, sep = "-"))) %>%
  drop_na(anomaly) %>%
  as_tsibble(index = date)
```

## Plot temperature anomalies

```r
temp %>%
  ggplot(aes(date, anomaly)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Global Temperature Anomalies",
       y = "Temperature Anomaly (C)", x = "Year")
```

---

# 4. Decomposition

```r
temp %>%
  model(STL(anomaly ~ season(window = "periodic"))) %>%
  components() %>%
  autoplot()
```

---

# 5. Forecasting CO2

```r
co2_fit <- co2_ts %>%
  model(ARIMA(trend))

report(co2_fit)

co2_forecast <- co2_fit %>%
  forecast(h = "5 years")

autoplot(co2_forecast, co2_ts)
```

---

# 6. Forecasting Temperature

```r
temp_fit <- temp %>%
  model(ARIMA(anomaly))

report(temp_fit)

temp_forecast <- temp_fit %>%
  forecast(h = "10 years")

autoplot(temp_forecast, temp)
```

---

# 7. Publishing to GitHub Pages

1. Knit this file to HTML.
2. Create a GitHub repository.
3. Add the HTML file.
4. In GitHub: Settings -> Pages.
5. Select the branch and folder containing the HTML.
6. Your site will appear at: https://yourusername.github.io/your-repo-name/

---

# Conclusion

You now have a workflow for importing, visualizing, decomposing, and forecasting environmental time series. This can be extended to precipitation, sea level, ENSO, Arctic ice, and more.
